title: Graded Array
prompt_md:
## Goal

Give a `GradeList` class (which stores 5 integer grades in a private array) a subscript operator so it supports array-style read and write access: `list[i]` for reading and `list[i] = v` for assigning.

## Input

- Line 1: five space-separated integers (0 <= each <= 100).
- Line 2: an integer `index` (0 <= index <= 4) and an integer `newGrade` (0 <= newGrade <= 100).

## Output

- Line 1: the grade at `index` before modification.
- Line 2: the grade at `index` after setting it to `newGrade`.

Provide a non-const `operator[]` returning `int&` (so assignment through the subscript modifies the stored element) and a const `operator[]` returning `int`. The provided code reads and writes the list exclusively through `operator[]`.

title: Name Lookup Table
prompt_md:
## Goal

Give a `NameTable` class (which stores strings in a fixed array) a const-correct subscript operator. A non-const table must allow both reading and writing through `table[i]`; a const table (accessed via `const NameTable&`) must allow reading only.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 10), the number of names.
- Next `n` lines: one name per line (no spaces).
- Last line: three integers `readIndex swapA swapB`.

## Output

- Line 1: the name at `readIndex`, read through a `const NameTable&`.
- Line 2: the name now at `swapA` after swapping the values at `swapA` and `swapB`.
- Line 3: the name now at `swapB` after that swap.

Provide a non-const `operator[]` returning `std::string&` and a const `operator[]` returning `const std::string&`. The provided `main` performs the read through a const reference and performs the swap through the non-const subscript.

test_cases.label: Swap first and last of many
